scope: security slice C — input parsing / deserialization
verdict_rationale: No CRITICAL injection / RCE / unsafe-deserialization defects; ruamel.yaml is correctly pinned to typ='rt' which is safe-by-default; JSON/TOML use stdlib parsers without object hooks; the JSONC tokenizer is string-aware and round-trip-safe. Two MAJOR DoS gaps (no YAML alias/anchor expansion cap, no input-size cap at any parser boundary) and several documented data-loss/footgun behaviours warrant follow-up but none are exploitable for code execution.
detailed_analysis: Phase 2.1's extraction of markdown_yaml_metadata_block.py collapsed five near-duplicate parse-preludes into one audit point covering claude_io, codex_io (skill), antigravity_io, opencode_io, rules_io, slash_command_io. That single point uses ruamel.yaml.YAML(typ='rt'), a direct derivative of the safe loader: it does NOT instantiate arbitrary Python objects from !!python/object, !!python/object/apply, !!python/name, or any other unsafe tag. No yaml.load without SafeLoader; no pickle; no eval; no marshal; no subprocess with shell=True. JSON and TOML are routed exclusively through json.loads and tomllib.loads with no object_hook, no parse_float=eval, no custom decoder. The JSONC handler's hand-rolled tokenizer was audited against every classical pitfall: // inside a string is preserved, \" does not terminate the string, trailing-comma inside a string is preserved, unterminated block comments eat to EOF and then json.loads rejects (no silent truncation, no infinite loop). The canonical and state loaders quarantine corrupt JSON. The schema-version check is strict equality. _LEGACY_REVIEW_MARKER strip is gated on schema_version < 4. extract_pair_id regex is anchored and linear. FRONTMATTER_RE uses lazy DOTALL but is bounded; no catastrophic backtracking. The portable archive zip importer reads only well-known entry names and validates every pair_id against validate_pair_id (canonical UUIDv4) before using it. mcp_secret_policy.py applies NFKC + casefold normalization defeating homoglyph and case-confusion attacks on field names. Two MAJOR DoS gaps: (1) no input-size cap at any parser boundary; (2) ruamel.yaml typ='rt' resolves anchors/aliases without expansion cap (quadratic billion-laughs variant remains feasible).

findings.description: yaml_load calls make_yaml().load(io.StringIO(text)) with typ='rt' and NO max_anchors, max_aliases, or document-size cap. ruamel.yaml's round-trip loader is RCE-safe but does resolve anchors and aliases. The quadratic billion-laughs pattern can drive parse time and memory to gigabytes on a sub-100KB input.
findings.exploit_scenario: A user installs a third-party Claude skill from an untrusted source. The skill's SKILL.md frontmatter contains nested anchor/alias references. split_frontmatter reaches dict(loaded) which forces materialization of the alias tree. The daemon's next poll hangs for minutes consuming all available RAM.
findings.recommendation: Cap input size before parse (reject frontmatter > 256 KB with AdapterParseError); cap document complexity by counting alias references during parse (subclass ruamel's Composer to track an aliases_resolved counter and raise after 10_000) or by switching to typ='safe' on the parse path.

findings.description: read_slots does shared_path.read_text(encoding='utf-8') with no size limit, then hands the entire text to format_handler.deserialize. The same shape exists at line 154 in _read_root_and_node. An attacker who controls a shared keyed-map file can drop a multi-gigabyte file there.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker writes 2 GB of {'_pad': 'a' * 2_000_000_000} to ~/.cursor/mcp.json. The daemon's next poll: read_text allocates 2 GB; json.loads allocates another 2 GB; format_handler.serialize(root) allocates a third copy. The host OOMs and kills the daemon, which on restart immediately repeats.
findings.recommendation: Cap read_text via path.stat().st_size check before opening (reject > 16 MB shared files); add a global MAX_PARSE_BYTES constant in formats/__init__.py and check it at the top of json_format.deserialize, toml_format.deserialize, and loads_slot.

findings.description: HIGH_CONFIDENCE_SECRET_VALUE_RE includes eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}. The {20,} and {10,} open-ended ranges over a character class can be O(n) per match attempt, and find_mcp_secret_literals calls this regex on EVERY string field in the document tree recursively.
findings.exploit_scenario: A hostile mcp.json slot contains 'eyJ' + 'A' * 10_000_000. The recursive walk visits this string; HIGH_CONFIDENCE_SECRET_VALUE_RE.search(value) scans 10 MB trying to find a . that never comes.
findings.recommendation: Bound the string length before applying the high-confidence regex: if len(value) > 4096: return False (legitimate JWTs are < 2 KB).

findings.description:
_strip_legacy_review_metadata truncates body content at the first occurrence of '

---
Converted Claude-specific metadata for manual review:' whenever prior_canonical is None. The marker is unique enough that accidental collision is unlikely, but an attacker who controls the artifact's first adoption can craft a Codex TOML whose developer_instructions end with the marker followed by hidden payload.
findings.exploit_scenario: Limited: a user copy-pasting prose from project documentation (which contains the marker verbatim) into a new Codex agent will lose the second half on adoption.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) gate the legacy strip on the source's adoption manifest version rather than prior_canonical is None, or (b) emit a WARNING log naming the truncated byte count whenever the strip fires.

findings.description: Every Markdown adapter coerces frontmatter values with bare str(...): claude_io.py:62-64, codex_io.py:283-285, antigravity_io.py:71-73, opencode_io.py:195,202,205, rules_io.py:66,69, slash_command_io.py:160,162,164. A YAML mapping for name (e.g. name: {a: 1}) becomes the literal string "{'a': 1}".
findings.exploit_scenario: User's .md contains name: [foo, bar]. The canonical is silently created with name="['foo', 'bar']". Across the next sync the Codex side gets name = "['foo', 'bar']", a filename slug of foo-bar.
findings.recommendation: Enforce string-ness at the parse boundary: if not isinstance(value, str): raise AdapterParseError(f'{label} frontmatter `name` must be a string, got {type(value).__name__}').

findings.description: Claude's claude_only bag stores hooks and mcpServers but ONLY checks the exact spellings ClaudeField.HOOKS and ClaudeField.MCP_SERVERS. If a user types MCPServers or mcp_servers, it lands in per_agentic_tool_extra['claude'] as a foreign field, and on cross-tool projection the canonical's own MCP_SERVERS slot is empty.
findings.exploit_scenario: A silent correctness regression. Two users collaborating: one types mcpServers, the other's tooling expects mcp_servers. The mismatch is invisible at parse time.
findings.recommendation: When folding per_agentic_tool_extra['claude'] for an unknown key, NFKC-normalize the key and compare against the known set case-insensitively; if a match is found, log a WARNING and route the value to the correct canonical slot.

findings.description: FRONTMATTER_RE bounded by file length but does a forward scan for \r?\n--- on every position. On a 100 MB Markdown file the regex linearly scans all 100 MB even when the frontmatter ends at byte 1000.
findings.exploit_scenario: User opens an editor that auto-saves a .md with hundreds of MB of base64-encoded image content in the body. The discovery walker reads the file, runs FRONTMATTER_RE.match against the whole text. Linear time but seconds-of-blocking per poll.
findings.recommendation: Cap the regex application to the first 64 KB: match = FRONTMATTER_RE.match(text[:65536]).

findings.category: Inconsistent exception type from parser boundary
findings.description: loads_slot raises bare ValueError('mcp_server JSON slot must be an object') while formats/json_format.py:20 raises bare ValueError('shared keyed-map root must be a JSON object') and markdown_yaml_metadata_block.py:135 raises AdapterParseError. Three different parser-error exception types which all need catching individually by upstream callers.
findings.exploit_scenario: Not a security attack on its own — a maintainability hazard. A future caller doing targeted parse-error handling will miss one.
findings.recommendation: Promote AdapterParseError (or rename to ParserError) to a package-wide base class in a neutral module; have json_format, toml_format, _slot_codec, and portable_archive all raise subclasses of it.

findings.description: codex_io._CORRUPTED_UTF8_BOM = '\u00ef\u00bb\u00bf' and markdown_yaml_metadata_block._CORRUPTED_UTF8_BOM = 'ï»¿' are byte-equivalent but the constant is duplicated rather than imported. formats/jsonc_tokenizer.strip_utf8_bom strips only the real BOM, not the corrupted-rendered-as-text form.
findings.exploit_scenario: Footgun. A Windows user copies an mcp.json between editors and the file ends up with the corrupted BOM. The daemon logs JSONDecodeError: Expecting value repeatedly with no hint about the BOM.
findings.recommendation: Move strip_bom_prefix from markdown_yaml_metadata_block.py to a neutral module handling both real and corrupted UTF-8 BOMs, and have formats/jsonc_tokenizer.strip_utf8_bom and codex_io._normalize_toml_text both call into it.
